title: Simple 2x4 Potting Bench with Slatted Back
summary: A potting bench with a work surface, bottom shelf, slatted back, and hutch shelf — from four 2x4s and ten 1x4s, braced with the only two angled cuts in the build.
description:
A potting bench is a workbench that's allowed to get dirty, and this one has the right anatomy: a waist-height work surface, a bottom shelf for bags of soil, a slatted back to hang tools against, and a hutch shelf up top for the things you reach for while both hands are muddy. The slats all run with gaps — soil falls through instead of piling up, which is the difference between a potting bench and a dirt shelf.

The frame is four 2x4 legs (back legs run full height to carry the back and hutch), 1x4 everything else, and exactly two angled cuts in the whole build: the 45° cross braces. Those braces matter more here than on most projects — a potting bench gets shoved sideways every time someone scoops from a soil bag on the shelf, and without the diagonals the racking works every screw loose in a season.

Step 1 carries nearly all the measuring in the project; it's marks on legs, and getting them right makes everything after it a screw-driving exercise. Build in cedar if it lives uncovered, or ordinary lumber with a good exterior finish under a roof overhang.

tools.note: Includes the two 45° brace cuts — mark with a speed square.
The leg marks in step 1 ARE the project — square lines, measured twice.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs and side aprons. Cedar if the bench lives in the open.

cutList.note: Full height — they carry the back boards and hutch shelf.

cutList.part: Front aprons, short shelf boards, back shelf
Long shelf boards and back boards

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 45°, long point to short point.

steps.title: Mark the legs and build the two sides
steps.body:
Mark the back legs at 11-1/4" and 35-1/4" up from the bottom — those are the top edges of the lower and upper side aprons. Attach the 22" side aprons to the back legs at the marks with 2-1/2" screws.

Then mark the front legs 1-1/2" in from the front edge and 11-1/4" up, and attach the other ends of the aprons. Build both sides, and check them against each other — all four apron heights must match or the shelves will teeter.

steps.title: Tie the sides together with the front aprons
steps.body: Screw and glue the 39" front aprons across the two side assemblies with the 2" screws — one at the work surface, one at the bottom shelf level. The bench is now freestanding; check it doesn't rock.

steps.title: Lay the shelves
steps.body:
Set the shelf boards on the bottom shelf with about 1/2" gaps and screw down with 2" screws. Repeat for the work surface.

The gaps are the design working: potting soil falls through instead of accumulating, and rain drains instead of pooling.

steps.title: Add the cross braces
steps.body:
Cut the two braces at 45° on both ends and screw and glue them from the back legs down to the back shelf board.

Don't skip these as cosmetic — every scoop of soil shoves this bench sideways, and the diagonals are what keep that from slowly racking the frame apart.

steps.title: Slat the back
steps.body: Screw the 42" back boards horizontally across the tall back legs with 2" screws, even gaps, from the work surface up. This slatted back is hanging real estate — trowels, snips, twine — so keep the gaps consistent enough to hook things over.

steps.title: Top it with the hutch shelf
steps.body:
Attach the hutch shelf at the top: screw through the back boards and the back legs into the shelf board's sides and back with 2" screws.

Sand everything — especially the work surface's front edge — and either let cedar weather or seal ordinary lumber well. Then pot something.

images.alt: Wooden potting bench with slatted work surface, bottom shelf, slatted back, and top hutch shelf
